spans.content: The teleportation protocol allows Alice to “send” an unknown qubit <sub>|</sub> <sub>i</sub> to Bob
without using a quantum channel, i.e. without physically sending the qubit, but
using only a classical channel, as for example a phone communication. To achieve
this, Alice and Bob share a pair of qubits in the entangled state
Alice entangles her qubit with the unknown qubit
teleport, using a CNOT gate. She then applies the Hadamard gate to the first
qubit (the qubit that originally was in the state <sub>|</sub> <sub>i</sub>) and measures the two qubits.
After this, Alice telephones the result of her meaurements to Bob. According to
the result 00, 01, 10, 11 communicated by Alice, Bob e↵ects one of four operations
, and by so doing transforms the state of his
qubit exactly in the state <sub>|</sub> <sub>i</sub> of the qubit originally owned by Alice.

```mermaid
graph LR
  t3 -->|X_Z["X\nZ"]| t3
  t3 -->|X_Z| t3
  t3 -->|X_Z| t3
  t3 -->|X_Z| t3
```
a 3-qubit circuit where the upper two qubits belong to Alice and the lower qubit
belongs to Bob. By following the evolution of the initial 3-qubit state
across the CNOT and H gates, one finds that the 3-qubit state before
A measurement by Alice produces a collapse into one of the four states contained in
this superposition. For example if Alice measures 01 the state (4.1) collapses into
This state is a product state, with Bob’s qubit in the state
Alice phones her result 01 to Bob, so that Bob learns that his qubit is in the
state (4.3). He therefore applies to it the gate X and reconstructs the original
The teleportation protocol allows Alice to “send” an unknown qubit <sub>|</sub> <sub>i</sub> to Bob
without using a quantum channel, i.e. without physically sending the qubit, but
using only a classical channel, as for example a phone communication. To achieve
this, Alice and Bob share a pair of qubits in the entangled state
Alice entangles her qubit with the unknown qubit
teleport, using a CNOT gate. She then applies the Hadamard gate to the first
qubit (the qubit that originally was in the state <sub>|</sub> <sub>i</sub>) and measures the two qubits.
After this, Alice telephones the result of her meaurements to Bob. According to
the result 00, 01, 10, 11 communicated by Alice, Bob e↵ects one of four operations
, and by so doing transforms the state of his
qubit exactly in the state <sub>|</sub> <sub>i</sub> of the qubit originally owned by Alice.

```mermaid
graph LR
  t3 -->|X_Z["X\nZ"]| t3
  t3 -->|X_Z| t3
  t3 -->|X_Z| t3
  t3 -->|X_Z| t3
```
a 3-qubit circuit where the upper two qubits belong to Alice and the lower qubit
belongs to Bob. By following the evolution of the initial 3-qubit state
across the CNOT and H gates, one finds that the 3-qubit state before
A measurement by Alice produces a collapse into one of the four states contained in
this superposition. For example if Alice measures 01 the state (4.1) collapses into
This state is a product state, with Bob’s qubit in the state
Alice phones her result 01 to Bob, so that Bob learns that his qubit is in the
state (4.3). He therefore applies to it the gate X and reconstructs the original
i) the state <sub>|</sub> <sub>i</sub> is teleported at a speed always
, since it is limited by the speed
ii) the original state <sub>|</sub> <sub>i</sub> owned by Alice gets destroyed (by measurement), so that
there is no violation of the no cloning theorem.
iii) teleportation is not vulnerable to noise, since it does not use a physical carrier.
(The classical part of the protocol, i.e. communication of Alice’ results to Bob,
could be disturbed by classical causes).
Quantum gates implement linear operations, and therefore can operate in parallel
on all the states contained in a superposition. Consider a Boolean function f , i.e.
a function taking N bits into 1 bit. Thus the domain of f is the set of binary N-bit
integers x and the image is <sub>{</sub>0, 1<sub>}</sub>. By <sub>|</sub>x<sub>i</sub> we denote the N-qubit state specified by
the string of 0’s and 1’s in x: thus for

one for each value of the variable x. But extracting this information
from the output state is not so easy. Measuring the N+1 qubit output state in
the computational basis yields a particular value for x, giving the corresponding
, and destroys all the rest of the information (all the other
values). We will see in next Section how interference may be used to recover more

ii) the original state <sub>|</sub> <sub>i</sub> owned by Alice gets destroyed (by measurement), so that
there is no violation of the no cloning theorem.
iii) teleportation is not vulnerable to noise, since it does not use a physical carrier.
(The classical part of the protocol, i.e. communication of Alice’ results to Bob,
could be disturbed by classical causes).
